summary: Gruber marks the historic election of Barack Obama on November 5, 2008, by quoting Hunter S. Thompson's despairing 1972 passage about Nixon's America. Thompson's quote laments that the country had fallen into the hands of 'greedy little hustlers' and questioned whether America could ever live up to its promise as 'a fantastic monument to all the best instincts of the human race.' Gruber answers Thompson's closing question — 'Where will it end?' — with two devastatingly simple lines: 'It ends here, today. I love this country.' The post uses the contrast between Thompson's cynicism and Obama's election to express profound optimism about America's trajectory.
key_points: The entire post is structured as a call-and-response across 36 years, with Thompson's despair answered by Obama's election
Gruber lets Thompson's prose do nearly all the heavy lifting, adding only 8 words of his own to deliver the emotional payoff
The implicit argument is that Obama's election redeems the promise Thompson feared America had permanently squandered
The post is a rare moment of unguarded emotion and patriotism from a writer known for ironic detachment
money_quotes: one of the few men who've run for President of the United States in this century who really understands what a fantastic monument to all the best instincts of the human race this country might have been
we are really just a nation of 220 million used car salesmen with all the money we need to buy guns, and no qualms at all about killing anybody else in the world who tries to make us uncomfortable
key_insight: By answering Hunter S. Thompson's 36-year-old despair with just eight words, Gruber frames Obama's election as the moment America finally lived up to the promise Thompson feared it had abandoned.
